interactions.summary: The student reads the Trojan lament over Hector, including Andromache's grief that Hector's death leaves her widowed, their son doomed, and Troy exposed to sack and enslavement.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Student reading Homer; seg-0066 is empty after boundary repair.

interactions.summary: Jiang says the Iliad ends with prophecy and perspective reversal: Greek readers are forced to imagine the future of Trojan women, children, and the burning city, and he compares that moral shock to imagining Gaza from the victim's side.

He closes by calling the Iliad a lifelong journey: trauma, pain, and suffering open empathy and wisdom, and a lifetime with the poem can put a universe in the soul before the class turns to the Odyssey.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Jiang closing lecture remarks; prompts for questions are rhetorical and no student question is captured.

speaker_notes.note: This segment is empty after boundary repair and should not be treated as a standalone utterance.
Jiang asks for questions, but no substantive student question is transcribed.

claims.claim: The Iliad's ending prophesies Troy's destruction: Hector's son will die, the men and children will be killed, and the women enslaved.
Homer forces Greek readers to imagine what it is like to be a Trojan woman facing the sack of her city, the murder of her family, and enslavement.
Jiang calls this forced perspective-switching the big bang of civilization because it violently assaults prejudice and opens access to the whole universe.
Only through trauma, pain, and suffering can one access empathy and wisdom; spending a life with the Iliad can give a person a universe in the soul.

signature_moments.moment: Civilization begins as a violent assault on your own consciousness: the victorious Greek is forced to feel the conquered Trojan woman's future.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This is the lecture's final and most public-facing formulation of why the Iliad matters.

signature_moments.moment: A lifetime of reading the Iliad can put a universe in the soul.
signature_moments.source_phrase: now has a universe in your soul
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It gives the episode its closing image and ties literature to spiritual enlargement.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: Jiang's phrase for the Iliad's forced switch into the enemy victim's perspective, which destroys prejudice and opens empathy.
The wisdom and interior vastness Jiang says can come from a lifetime of reading the Iliad.

chronology_notes.note: The lecture explicitly brings the Iliad's victim-perspective reversal into comparison with Gaza/Palestine in the contemporary political present of 2026-01-28.

uncertainty_notes.note: The contemporary Gaza analogy is Jiang's classroom comparison; downstream public read should preserve chronology and avoid expanding beyond the transcript.
